flashcards.back: Wind energy is the kinetic energy derived from moving air masses, captured and converted into electrical energy using wind turbines. It is a clean, sustainable, and renewable energy source.
Wind power is the rate at which wind energy is captured by a turbine, typically measured in watts or kilowatts. It depends on air density, rotor swept area, and the cube of wind velocity.
The Betz Limit (59.3%) is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a wind turbine. No wind turbine can convert more than 59.3% of kinetic energy in wind into mechanical energy.
Wind Power Density is the power available per unit area, measured in W/m². It is calculated as ½ρv³, where ρ is air density and v is wind velocity.

flashcards.front: Name two types of wind turbines.
flashcards.back: Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT). HAWTs have blades rotating around a horizontal axis, while VAWTs rotate around a vertical axis.
Cut-in wind speed is the minimum wind speed (typically 3-4 m/s) at which a wind turbine starts generating usable power. Below this speed, the turbine is shut down to prevent damage.
Rated wind speed is the wind speed at which the turbine reaches its maximum power output. Beyond this speed, power output is controlled to protect the turbine (typically 12-15 m/s).
Cut-out wind speed (or furling speed) is the maximum wind speed (typically 25 m/s) at which turbines are shut down to prevent structural damage. Turbines automatically brake and shut down.
Capacity Factor is the ratio of actual energy output over a period to the maximum possible output if the turbine operated at rated capacity continuously. Typically 20-40% for wind turbines.
Hub height is the vertical distance from the ground to the center of the rotor (the hub) of a wind turbine. Higher hub heights access stronger and more consistent winds.
Wind shear is the variation in wind speed and direction with height. Wind speed generally increases with height due to reduced surface friction, affecting turbine performance.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of offshore wind farms?
flashcards.back: Offshore wind farms have higher and more consistent wind speeds, reduced noise and visual impact, larger space for installation, and higher capacity factors compared to onshore installations.
Rotor swept area is the circular area through which the turbine blades rotate, calculated as πr² where r is the blade length. This determines how much wind energy can be captured.
Tip Speed Ratio is the ratio of blade tip speed to wind speed. It determines turbine efficiency and is optimized differently for HAWT (typically 6-7) and VAWT (typically 3-4).
